mnemonics.title: Paxos Phases Remember
mnemonics.content: PPAL: Proposer Proposes, Acceptors Accept, Learners Learn the agreed value. Order: Prepare first (promise), then Accept (agreement), finally Learn (inform).

mnemonics.title: Raft Leader Election
mnemonics.content: ELECTION: Everyone Times Out, Becomes Candidate, Votes for Self, Requests Votes, Majority Wins, Becomes Leader. If tie, new timeout and retry.

mnemonics.title: Byzantine Node Formula
mnemonics.content: BYZANTINE: Big Yields Zero Tolerance - Need 3f+1 nodes to handle f traitors. Think: 1 traitor needs 4 nodes, 2 traitors need 7 nodes.

mnemonics.title: CAP Theorem
mnemonics.content: CAP: Consistency vs Availability during Partition - Pick two, one must go. Remember: P is always present, choose C or A.

acronyms.usage: Proves consensus is impossible in asynchronous systems with even one faulty process
Atomic commitment protocol with Prepare and Commit phases for distributed transactions
Non-blocking atomic commit protocol adding Pre-commit phase to 2PC
Consensus algorithm tolerating Byzantine failures with 3f+1 nodes
Technique for fault-tolerant services by replicating state machines across nodes
Understandable consensus algorithm with leader-based approach

acronyms.expansion: Greek letter Ξ (xi) - named after the Greek island
acronyms.usage: Family of consensus algorithms for achieving agreement in distributed systems

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a circle with n nodes. Draw a line dividing it exactly in half - any group larger than half (n/2+1) can see each other. For 5 nodes, any 3 nodes form a quorum that always overlaps with any other quorum.

Draw three columns: Proposer (left), Acceptors (middle), Learners (right). Arrows flow: Proposer sends Prepare (arrow down), Acceptors promise, then Proposer sends Accept (arrow), Acceptors accept, then Learners get the decision (arrow).
Horizontal timeline with vertical markers: Term starts → Follower timeout → Candidate state → Vote request sent → Majority votes received → Leader elected → Heartbeats sent → Term continues.
Draw a network split vertically. Left side has Leader A, right side has Leader B (both think they are leader). Draw an X through the middle showing conflict. Only one should exist.

analogies.analogy: Like a group of friends deciding where to eat dinner. Everyone must agree on one restaurant despite some friends being unreliable or communication being slow. The 'restaurant' is the agreed value.
Like a military unit where some soldiers might be traitors. Loyal soldiers need enough numbers (3f+1 total) to outvote the traitors and agree on a coordinated attack plan.
Like a parliament passing a law: Proposer suggests a bill, Acceptors vote on it, and once majority approves (Learn phase), it becomes law that everyone follows.

Like choosing a class representative. If the current representative is absent too long (timeout), students (followers) nominate themselves (become candidates) and vote. Winner becomes the new representative.
Like a group project where the coordinator (teacher) asks everyone to prepare, then leaves before saying 'submit'. Everyone waits indefinitely - the project is stuck/blocked.
Imagine two committees with members. Any two committees must have at least one person in common to ensure information sharing. That's why any two quorums in consensus must intersect.
